data.title: Mergers and acquisitions
data.description:
The following bids, mergers, acquisitions and disposals were reported by 1930 GMT on Friday:

** Germany's banking watchdog (BaFin) on Friday said it had ordered the Italian lender UniCredit to stop improper advertising related to its attempt to take over Commerzbank .

** British product testing firm Intertek rejected a revised takeover bid of 54 pounds per share from Swedish private equity group EQT AB on Friday, saying the proposal fundamentally undervalued the company and its prospects.

** French automotive group Cosmobilis is in talks to buy the commercial rights to the World Rally Championship (WRC), two people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

** Finnish forestry group UPM-Kymmene and South African-listed wood fibre producer Sappi face a lengthy EU antitrust investigation into their planned graphic paper joint venture, as regulators raise competition concerns, people with direct knowledge of the matter said.

** Porsche will sell its stakes in sports car makers Bugatti and Rimac to a consortium led by a U.S. fund linked to Egypt's billionaire Sawiris family, the companies said, as Porsche's new CEO focuses on reviving its struggling core brand.

** Canadian AI firm Cohere agreed to buy German tech startup Aleph Alpha at an undisclosed price as it looks to increase sales to government and business customers in highly regulated European markets.

** Spanish conglomerate Acciona is exploring a sale of Bestinver that could value the asset manager at about 600 million euros ($700.92 million), Spanish newspaper Expansion reported on Friday, citing sources familiar with the matter.

** French energy major TotalEnergies agreed to invest in a Kazakhstan-based onshore wind and energy storage project valued at $1.2 billion, with roughly 75% of it financed externally, the company said.

** Monte dei Paschi di Siena could consider parting with its stake in insurer Generali as part of future development plans, but would only do so if the Rome government approves of the buyer, a source briefed on the matter said.

data.title: Maine governor rejects first US state freeze on new data centers
data.description:
The Democratic governor of Maine, Janet Mills, on Friday vetoed a bill that would have made it the first ​U.S. state to impose a moratorium on large new data centers, even as local ‌opposition to the electricity-hungry facilities grows.

If it had been signed into law, the bill would have frozen approvals until October 2027 for data centers requiring more than 20 megawatts ​of power, while a state-appointed council analyzed ⁠their impact on the local grid, electricity bills, air and water.

In a letter to the Maine legislature, Mills said she supports a temporary moratorium on data center projects - and would have signed the bill if it had included an exemption for a data center project underway in the town of Jay.

"A moratorium is appropriate given the impacts of massive data centers in other states on the environment and on electricity rates. But the final version of this bill fails to allow for a specific project in the Town of Jay that enjoys strong local support from its host community and region," Mills said.

The Androscoggin paper mill shut down in 2023 after a boiler explosion, leading to hundreds of job losses.

Work to develop a $550 million data center on that site is expected to create more than 800 construction jobs and at least 100 high-paying permanent jobs, and would contribute property tax revenue to the town of Jay, Mills said.

The decision on Friday reflects the difficult trade-off facing political leaders who must weigh data centers' impact on the environment and household energy bills against the millions of dollars in investment and tax revenue they can bring.

Mills also said that she plans to issue an executive order establishing a council to examine the impact of data centers in Maine and has signed a bill to prohibit data center projects from Maine's business development tax incentive programs.

American tech giants have pledged to spend more than $600 billion on artificial intelligence data centers this year as part of a spending spree that has boosted the U.S. economy and is considered the biggest since the telecom boom of the late 1990s.

But mounting opposition to that buildout has led at least 11 U.S. states to weigh legislation that would halt or restrain development of the facilities, even as the Trump administration pressures states to stay out of AI regulation.

To ease worries about rising electricity bills, Washington last month got big technology companies to sign a ​voluntary pledge at ​the White House ⁠that they would bear the cost of new electricity generation to power their data centers.

Two Democratic lawmakers - Senator Bernie Sanders and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez - have also introduced legislation to halt all construction on data centers until Congress passes AI ⁠safety legislation.

data.title: Wheat Futures Fall on Profit-Taking Push — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for July delivery fell 0.6% to $6.16 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, in response to profit-taking among traders.
  • Corn for July delivery fell 0.1% to $4.63 1/2 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for July delivery rose 0.2% to $11.76 3/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Locking in Gains: Traders locked in profits in grains ahead of the weekend, keeping futures down for most of the session. There was speculation about whether the market added too much risk premium to futures too early in the year. "Open interest was getting a little high in corn/soy and profits were taken," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. The CFTC will update its Commitments of Traders report this afternoon. Last week's report showed both corn and soybeans had comfortably net-long positions among fund traders.

Sleepy Activity: Volumes traded on the CBOT today were light, with price movement mostly listless as of Friday afternoon, said AgResource in a note. "Interest in trading is dull with rallies capped by favorable Central U.S. weather with a forecast improvement for Plains rainfall," said the firm.

INSIGHT

Question Mark: Wet weather in the eastern Corn Belt has kept a lot of farmers out of their fields so far this spring. This may eventually lift futures if things don't dry up and allow farmers to plant, said Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. If the rain breaks, then grains are likely to keep trading sideways, he said. "Look for the market to continue its range-bound trade with potential for seasonal weakness as the crop goes in, and before focus shifts to summer weather," said Bergman.

AHEAD

-The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.

-The USDA will release its weekly Crop Progress report at 4 p.m. ET Monday.

-The EIA will release its Weekly Petroleum Status Update report at 10:30 a.m. ET Wednesday.

Matt Grossman contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Treasuries Move Modestly Higher After Early Volatility
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Treasuries saw considerable volatility early in the session, treasuries moved modestly higher over the course of the trading day on Friday.

After bouncing back and forth across the unchanged line in morning trading, bond prices spent the afternoon hovering above the unchanged line. Subsequently, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, dipped 1.3 basis points to 4.310 percent.

The modest strength among treasuries came in reaction to a pullback by the price of crude oil, which has given back ground after skyrocketing over the past few sessions.

U.S. crude oil futures are slumping by more than 1 percent after a report from Reuters said Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi will arrive in Pakistan tonight for peace talks with the U.S.

Araghchi himself mentioned through X that his trip is focused on bilateral matters and regional developments though he did not give much detail.

According to CNN, U.S. Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner will likely represent the U.S. though Vice President JD Vance will step in if needed.

President Donald Trump's announcement of a three-week extension of the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon has also weighed on crude oil prices.

On the U.S. economic front, revised data released by the University of Michigan showed consumer sentiment in the U.S. deteriorated by less than previously estimated in April but still hit a record low.

The University of Michigan said its consumer sentiment index for April was upwardly revised to 49.8 from the preliminary reading of 47.6. Economists had expected the index to be unrevised.

Despite the upward revision, the consumer sentiment index is still down from 53.3 in March and at its lowest reading on records going back to 1952.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: ALR: Q1 2026 profit fell amid higher legal risks and tax rates, despite loan growth
data.description:

data.title: AAR completes $35M acquisition of Aircraft Reconfig Technologies, adds ODA and STC capability
data.description:
AAR completed the $35 million cash acquisition of Aircraft Reconfig Technologies, adding FAA ODA and enhanced certification capabilities.

Key Highlights:

  • AAR purchased Aircraft Reconfig Technologies for $35 million in cash, subject to customary post-closing adjustments.
  • The acquisition adds FAA Organization Designation Authorization, enabling AAR to issue STCs and PMAs without third parties.
  • The deal immediately expands AAR’s engineering and certification offerings for aircraft cabin interiors.
  • Transaction executed by AAR Aircraft Services, strengthening Repair & Engineering segment capabilities.

Original SEC Filing: AAR CORP [ AIR ] - 8-K - Apr. 24, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Charter's stock is getting hammered. Here's what's fueling its worst day on record.
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

Charter lost more internet subscribers than expected in the latest quarter, signaling that the company isn't out of the woods when it comes to broadband woes

Charter Communications, which operates its cable and broadband services under the name Spectrum, said it expects its deal to acquire Cox Communications to close by the summer.

It's a tough time to be a cable and broadband operator.

Charter Communications (CHTR) reported steep declines in both its broadband and cable customer bases in the first quarter, sending its stock into a tailspin as it moves to close a $34.5 billion acquisition of Cox Communications.

Shares were down 25% in afternoon trading following the earnings report, in which the company reported a greater-than-expected drop in broadband subscribers as well as lower-than-expected earnings per share. The stock drop is on pace to be Charter's worst daily performance on record.

Shares of cable peer Comcast (CMCSA) are down more than 11% in sympathy, even though that company's own earnings report was well-received a day earlier.

Charter said it ended the quarter with a net 120,000 fewer broadband customers compared with the prior quarter. That was steeper than the 59,000 customers it lost in the same quarter a year earlier and also worse than the estimated decline of 100,000 predicted by analysts.

That decline suggested that Charter still has work to do when it comes to stabilizing its business in the face of changing customer habits.

"Charter may be some ways away from adding subscribers, but a reasonable objective should be fewer year-on-year net losses," analyst Craig Moffett of MoffettNathanson Research wrote in a note to clients. "They're not there yet."

Traditional cable companies have struggled as AT&T (T), Verizon Communications (VZ) and T-Mobile US (TMUS) encroach on their territory with internet offerings at both the high and low ends. While Charter is pushing to bundle wireless service with its more established offerings, that part of the business is "growing rapidly, but a little slower than expected," Moffett wrote.

Charter also reported a loss of 60,000 cable subscribers for its Spectrum service in the quarter, which marked an improvement from the 180,000 it lost in the same quarter in 2025. The company attributed that to "simplified pricing and packaging and benefits from the inclusion of programmers' streaming applications in Spectrum's expanded basic video packages."

The company said that it expected its average revenue per user - a key industry metric - to be flat for the year.

Charter remains the largest cable provider in the country, with 12.5 million subscribers as of the end of the first quarter, but industry watchers expect it to soon be surpassed by YouTube TV, which had an estimated 10 million subscribers late last year.

The company reported revenue of $13.6 billion in the quarter, down 1% compared with the same period of the previous year but in line with expectations. The company posted net income of $1.16 billion in the quarter, down from $1.22 billion the year earlier. That translated to earnings per share of $9.17, below the $9.96 predicted by analysts.

The disappointing earnings report comes as Charter moves toward closing the $34.5 billion deal it reached last year to acquire Cox Communications.

"We've now received all the necessary federal and state approvals that we need to close, except from California, and we're working with the California Public Utilities Commission towards the summer close," Charter CEO Christopher Winfrey said on a call with analysts.

The deal comes at a time of deep unsteadiness for the cable industry, as customers are increasingly cutting the cord and moving to streaming video services. There are now about 60 million cable subscribers in the U.S., down from around 100 million a decade ago.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: TANLA: Revenue grew year-over-year, dividend declared, and tax demand disclosed as contingent liability
data.description:
Audited results show year-over-year growth in consolidated revenue and stable net profit. A second interim dividend of ₹6 per share was declared, and a share buyback was completed. A tax demand was disclosed as a contingent liability, with management expecting a favorable outcome.

Original document: Tanla Platforms Ltd [TANLA] Interim report — Apr. 27 2026

data.title: TANLA: FY26 revenue up 9.7% YoY, with strong Q4 growth, margin gains, and robust cash position
data.description:
Revenue grew 9.7% YoY in FY26, with strong Q4 performance and margin expansion. Both Digital Platforms and Enterprise Communications contributed to growth, supported by innovation and customer expansion. Cash position remains robust and the company is debt-free.

Original document: Tanla Platforms Ltd [TANLA] Slides Release — Apr. 27 2026
